source: All-In Podcast — E221: AI cold war, Signalgate, CoreWeave IPO, tariffs
summary: Deregulation is the highest-confidence policy lever for economic growth; tariffs carry execution risk but the administration will adapt if they fail
summary_extended: Baker argues that deregulation is the single highest-confidence policy lever available to the current administration for driving economic growth, and that it represents the one area of genuine alignment between Wall Street and Washington. He contends that every tariff announcement should be paired with deregulation messaging two or three times over, in order to offset the uncertainty and execution risk that tariffs introduce. Baker credits Treasury Secretary Bessent with providing a coherent theoretical framework for the administration's economic strategy, and believes the administration will pivot quickly if tariffs prove counterproductive, with the midterm elections serving as the hard political deadline forcing accountability. He identifies recession as the one outcome the administration cannot afford, making it the key forcing function that would trigger a policy course-correction on tariffs.
detail: Baker argues that deregulation is the one policy where Wall Street and the administration agree, and that every tariff announcement should be paired with deregulation messaging. He believes the administration has a coherent theory of the case (citing Bessent) and will adapt quickly if tariffs don't work — pointing to the midterms as the political forcing function. He identifies recession as the one outcome they cannot afford.
confidence_evidence: Baker frames this as his 'one thought for the administration' and elaborates the political logic, but it is macro/policy commentary rather than a direct investment thesis.
quote_fragment: every time they say the word tariff they need to say the word deregulation two or three times
